duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: sqrt(0) executes the EVM stop() opcode instead of returning packedFloat(0), silently halting the entire execution context with success status, causing partial state commitment and transaction corruption
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All 8 threats describe the identical code defect: `if iszero(a) { stop() }` in Float128.sqrt(). They differ only in framing (DoS vs state desync vs reentrancy-like) but target the same single assembly instruction and its consequences.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: eq() uses raw uint256 bitwise comparison instead of mathematical value comparison, returning false for identical values encoded in different M/L precision modes
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All 5 threats target the same single line: `retVal = packedFloat.unwrap(a) == packedFloat.unwrap(b)` in eq(). They describe identical root cause (bitwise comparison ignoring M/L encoding) with different economic framing.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: le()/ge() use strict lt/gt instead of lte/gte for equal-exponent comparisons, plus combined comparison system failure (trichotomy violation) for M/L encoded equal values
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: Core defect is le() using `lt(aMan,bMan)` and ge() using `lt(bMan,aMan)` instead of inclusive comparisons. The combined/trichotomy threats (ECON-H002, ECON2-H009) describe the emergent failure from this plus eq() bug. ECON2-H010 (toPackedFloat divergent encodings) and ECON2-H013 (divL forcing L-encoding) describe trigger mechanisms for the same comparison failure. TECH-H016 and OPS-H011 describe lt/gt concerns that are consequences of the same M/L encoding design flaw. ECON-H002 is the most comprehensive, covering all five comparison operators with CRITICAL severity and economic impact.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: ln() ignores the sign bit when extracting mantissa, silently computing ln(|x|) for negative inputs instead of reverting, violating the mathematical domain constraint
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All 12 threats describe the identical defect: `mantissa := and(input, MANTISSA_MASK)` in ln() strips the sign bit without checking it. Variants describe different economic consequences (bonding curve inversion, pricing manipulation, domain bypass) but the root cause is the same missing sign validation. Note: TECH2-H003 and TECH2-H004 each have duplicate IDs with threats in other groups; the instances removed here are the ln-negative variants (titles containing 'ln() accepts negative').

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: EVM exp(10, adj) opcode wraps modulo 2^256 for adj > 77 in add/sub mantissa alignment, producing garbage divisor and corrupted arithmetic results
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All threats describe the same `sdiv(bMan, exp(BASE, adj))` overflow in add()/sub(). ECON-H003 is most comprehensive, uniquely identifying both the sdiv sign error at adj=77 AND the exp overflow at adj>=78. ECON-H006 describes an exploitation pattern (asymmetric deposit/withdraw) that depends entirely on this same root cause. Note: TECH2-H005 duplicate ID — the instance removed here is the 'add/sub exp wraparound' variant.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: Unsigned exponent subtraction underflow in add/sub when raw exponent < MAX_DIGITS_M (38), producing massive incorrect exponent via uint256 wraparound
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All 5 threats target the identical instruction: `r := sub(aExp, shl(EXPONENT_BIT, MAX_DIGITS_M))` which wraps for rawExp < 38. TECH-H006 has the most detailed technical analysis. Note: TECH2-H006 duplicate ID — the instance removed here is the 'Exponent Underflow in add/sub' variant.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: mul() exponent arithmetic overflow/underflow: result exponent computed via unchecked subtraction can exceed 14-bit field range in both directions, producing catastrophically wrong magnitude
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All threats target the same formula: `rExp := sub(add(shr(EXPONENT_BIT, aExp), shr(EXPONENT_BIT, bExp)), ZERO_OFFSET)` in mul(). TECH-H007/OPS-H006/OPS2-H012 describe overflow (large exponents); OPS-H008/TECH2-H007/ECON2-H007/ECON-H010 describe underflow (small exponents). ECON-H005 covers both mul and div overflow. OPS-H014 is a general exponent-field wrap observation. All stem from the same lack of bounds checking on rExp. ECON-H010 (CRITICAL) best captures the severity. Note: TECH2-H007 duplicate ID — instance removed here is 'mul exponent underflow'.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: div() exponent calculation underflow via unchecked `(aExp + ZERO_OFFSET) - bExp` when dividing small by large numbers, producing massive incorrect exponent
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All 4 threats target the identical unchecked subtraction in div(). ECON2-H008 (HIGH) has the best economic impact analysis and clearest description of exploitation.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: sqrt() Heron's method branch exponent underflow: `sub(aExp, MAX_DIGITS_M)` wraps for small raw exponents, then unsigned div by 2 on wrapped value produces massive incorrect result exponent
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All 3 threats target the identical instruction `aExp := sub(aExp, MAX_DIGITS_M)` in sqrt()'s else branch. TECH-H008 has the most detailed proof of signal referencing the full chain of operations.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: Complex normalization branching in add/sub may produce denormalized packedFloat with L-flag/mantissa-size mismatch, corrupting all downstream arithmetic
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: Both threats describe the same concern: edge cases in add/sub normalization logic where _isM flag, digit-count checks, and L-flag assignment could produce a mantissa with wrong number of digits relative to the L-flag. ECON-H007 has stronger economic impact assessment (up to 10^34 magnitude error).

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: Non-canonical zero values (mantissa=0 with non-zero exponent/sign/flag bits) bypass zero-detection checks and propagate malformed state through arithmetic and comparison operations
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: All 7 threats describe aspects of the same design flaw: zero is only detected via `packedFloat.unwrap(x)==0`, so non-canonical zeros bypass all zero checks. TECH-H018/TECH2-H006(decode variant)/ECON2-H017 focus on decode() accepting these values; ECON-H009/OPS-H013/OPS2-H006 focus on arithmetic propagation; ECON-H012 describes the most severe impact (balance destruction). Note: TECH2-H006 duplicate ID — the instance removed here is the 'decode non-canonical exponent' variant.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: ln() DoS: recursive call in ln_helper for inputs >1 may not terminate if reciprocal computation retriggers the recursive branch; also ln(0) causes division-by-zero revert without descriptive error
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: TECH-H015 covers both the recursion risk and the div-by-zero for mantissa=0. ECON-H008 and OPS2-H011 focus on the recursion aspect. TECH2-H010 focuses on ln(0) div-by-zero. All share the same ln_helper code path. ECON-H008 has the most detailed DoS impact analysis.

duplicate_groups.vulnerability_description: toPackedFloat() does not validate that the final exponent fits within the 14-bit field, allowing out-of-range exponents to wrap/truncate during packing, producing packedFloat with drastically wrong magnitude
duplicate_groups.removal_reason: Both threats target the same missing bounds check in toPackedFloat(): `shl(EXPONENT_BIT, add(exponent, ZERO_OFFSET))` without verifying the result fits in 14 bits. ECON-H017 (HIGH) provides clearer economic exploitation scenario (microscopic value inflated to astronomical).
